md: Up to this point we've been **evaluating** trig functions: 'find $\sin 30°$.' Here we flip the question around. We're given a value and asked: **for which angle(s) does the trig function equal that value?** That's a **trigonometric equation**.
**1. There are usually infinitely many solutions** (because trig functions are periodic).
**2. Inverse trig functions** ($\sin^{-1}$, $\cos^{-1}$, $\tan^{-1}$) give us the *one* answer in a chosen range — the **principal value** — and we use the periodicity to find the rest.

Solving $\sin x = c$ means: find every $x$ where the sine curve crosses the horizontal line $y = c$. The curve is wiggly and infinite — it crosses any horizontal line in $[-1, 1]$ infinitely many times.
Drag the green line in the widget below. Every red dot is a solution. Watch how the count changes as you cross $y = 1$ or $y = -1$.

blocks.title: Solutions per period
blocks.body: **$\sin x = c$ and $\cos x = c$:** if $-1 < c < 1$, *two* solutions per period of $2\pi$. If $c = \pm 1$, *one* solution per period. If $|c| > 1$, *no* solutions. **$\tan x = c$:** *one* solution per period of $\pi$ for any $c$ (tan is unbounded).

md: ## 2. Solving Trig Equations with Special Values
When $c$ is a special-angle value ($0, \tfrac{1}{2}, \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}, \tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, 1$), you can find exact solutions using the unit circle and reference angles.
**Find all quadrants where $\sin > 0$.** That's Q I and Q II (ASTC: All–Sin in upper half).
**Build solutions in each quadrant** using the reference angle:
**Sign is negative**, so $\cos < 0$ — that's Q II and Q III.

blocks.title: Three-step recipe
blocks.body: **Step 1.** Find the reference angle from $|c|$ (using a special-angle table or $\sin^{-1}, \cos^{-1}, \tan^{-1}$). **Step 2.** Identify the quadrants where the trig function has the right sign (use ASTC). **Step 3.** Use the reference angle and the quadrant rules to write each solution.

md: Often the problem asks for *every* solution, not just those in $[0, 2\pi)$. Add multiples of the period to extend.
**For $\sin x = c$ or $\cos x = c$**: take your two solutions in $[0, 2\pi)$ — call them $x_1$ and $x_2$ — and write the **general solution** as:
$$x = x_1 + 2\pi k \quad\text{or}\quad x = x_2 + 2\pi k, \qquad k \in \mathbb{Z}$$
**For $\tan x = c$**: take the one solution in $[0, \pi)$ — call it $x_1$ — and:
(Period of tan is $\pi$, not $2\pi$, because the curve repeats every $\pi$.)
### Worked example 3 — general solution for $\sin x = \tfrac{1}{2}$
for any integer $k$. (For $k = 0$ you recover the originals; $k = 1$ gives $\tfrac{13\pi}{6}$ and $\tfrac{17\pi}{6}$; $k = -1$ gives $-\tfrac{11\pi}{6}$ and $-\tfrac{7\pi}{6}$.)
When $c$ isn't a special-angle value (e.g., $\sin x = 0.4$), the answer isn't a clean fraction of $\pi$. We use the **inverse trig functions** to get a numerical approximation:
These are the inverse functions you've already used informally — but they have one important wrinkle that makes them genuine functions: **restricted domains/ranges**.

$\sin x$ isn't one-to-one over its full domain — many different $x$ values give the same sine. To make an inverse function (which must give *one* output per input), we restrict the domain of sine to a piece where it's monotonic. Same for the others.
$\sin^{-1}: [-1, 1] \to [-\tfrac{\pi}{2}, \tfrac{\pi}{2}]$ — output is in Q IV or Q I.
$\cos^{-1}: [-1, 1] \to [0, \pi]$ — output is in Q I or Q II.
$\tan^{-1}: \mathbb{R} \to (-\tfrac{\pi}{2}, \tfrac{\pi}{2})$ — output is in Q IV or Q I.

blocks.title: Principal value
blocks.body: $\sin^{-1}(c)$ returns the **principal value** — the *one* angle in $[-\tfrac{\pi}{2}, \tfrac{\pi}{2}]$ whose sine is $c$. To find *all* angles whose sine is $c$, you start from the principal value and use the periodicity / symmetry rules. Same idea for cos and tan.

md: $\sin^{-1}(x)$ is the inverse function — same thing as $\arcsin(x)$. **Do not** read it as $1/\sin(x)$ — that's $\csc(x)$ (cosecant). Same for $\cos^{-1}$ vs $\sec$, and $\tan^{-1}$ vs $\cot$.
**Notation can confuse:** $\sin^2(x)$ does mean $(\sin x)^2$. But $\sin^{-1}(x)$ does *not* mean $(\sin x)^{-1}$. The historical notation is just unfortunate.
**Other solution in $[0, 2\pi)$:** sine is also positive in Q II.
**Principal value:** $\cos^{-1}(-0.3) \approx 1.875$ rad ($\approx 107.5°$). Note that $\cos^{-1}$'s range is $[0, \pi]$ — so this is already in Q II, where cosine is negative.
**Second solution** (cos is also negative in Q III): reflect across $\pi$:
Equations involving $\csc$, $\sec$, or $\cot$ are easiest to solve by **flipping to the basic three** using the reciprocal identities.

blocks.tex: \csc x = c \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \sin x = \dfrac{1}{c} \qquad \sec x = c \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \cos x = \dfrac{1}{c} \qquad \cot x = c \;\Longleftrightarrow\; \tan x = \dfrac{1}{c}

md: **Solve.** Reference angle $\tfrac{\pi}{3}$. Cosine positive in Q I and Q IV.
## 6. Trigonometric Equations That Need Algebra First
Sometimes you have to massage the equation algebraically before applying inverse trig.
$\Rightarrow 2\sin x = 1 \Rightarrow \sin x = \tfrac{1}{2}$. Then proceed as in Worked Example 1: $x = \tfrac{\pi}{6}, \tfrac{5\pi}{6}$.

Take square root: $\sin x = \pm \tfrac{1}{2}$. Solve each case separately (each has 2 solutions in $[0, 2\pi)$). Total: 4 solutions: $\tfrac{\pi}{6}, \tfrac{5\pi}{6}, \tfrac{7\pi}{6}, \tfrac{11\pi}{6}$.

blocks.title: Recap
blocks.body: **Solving trig equations** = finding all $x$ where $f(x) = c$. Use **reference angle + quadrant signs (ASTC)** for solutions in $[0, 2\pi)$. Add multiples of the period for the **general solution**. **Inverse trig functions** ($\sin^{-1}, \cos^{-1}, \tan^{-1}$) return the **principal value** — one angle in a restricted range. Use that as a starting point, not the final answer. **Reciprocal equations** flip to the basic three first. **Algebraic manipulation** comes before trig: isolate the trig function on one side.
